trade.summary: Bilateral trade collapsed following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 and the application of EU sanctions. Italy was historically one of Russia's most important EU trade partners, especially in energy, luxury goods, and machinery. Energy trade has been almost entirely replaced. Some residual trade in non-sanctioned goods continues. Italian luxury goods, food, and manufacturing exports to Russia dropped sharply.

diplomatic.summary: Italy applies all EU sanctions on Russia and has politically aligned with the Western position on Ukraine, though Italy has historically been among the EU members most inclined toward dialogue with Russia. Prime Minister Meloni's government maintained the Western consensus on Ukraine support while some coalition partners (Salvini's Lega) have been more sympathetic to Russia. Italy expelled Russian diplomats as part of coordinated EU actions. Relations are frozen at the EU-mandated level of minimal engagement.
recent_incidents: Italy providing military equipment to Ukraine

military.summary: No military cooperation. Italy has provided military assistance to Ukraine within NATO frameworks. Russia views Italy as a hostile state within the Western alliance. NATO designation makes bilateral military ties impossible.

financial.summary: Financial ties severed by EU sanctions. Italian banks and financial institutions have exited Russian market. Frozen Russian assets in Italy. Italian subsidiaries in Russia faced nationalization risk. Significant losses for Italian firms with Russian exposure (Unicredit notably).

energy.summary: Energy ties, once Italy's primary dependency on Russia, have been almost entirely severed. Italy replaced Russian gas with Norwegian, Algerian, Azerbaijani, and LNG supplies. ENI and Gazprom contracts terminated or allowed to lapse. Italy's gas dependency on Russia fell from ~40% in 2021 to near zero by 2024.

scientific.summary: Scientific cooperation largely suspended since 2022. Individual academic contacts continue in some fields (physics, mathematics) but institutional programs have been paused or cancelled.
